rules: Every screen must answer: what is this, why it matters, what to do next.
Headlines must clearly describe the function or value of the page.
Avoid ambiguous labels like 'Click here' or 'Learn more' without context.
Use familiar wording over clever or creative wording.
Primary actions must be visually distinguishable.

consistency.description: Similar elements should behave and appear the same.
rules: Buttons with the same purpose must share identical styles.
Navigation patterns should remain consistent across pages.
Icons must represent the same meaning everywhere.
Spacing and typography scales must remain consistent.
Design patterns should not change unexpectedly between sections.

visual_hierarchy.description: Guide user attention using size, contrast, spacing and alignment.
rules: Each page must have a clear primary element.
Headings must follow a logical order: H1 > H2 > H3.
Primary actions must visually stand out.
Use size and contrast to indicate importance.
Important content must appear above secondary content.

simplicity.description: Reduce complexity and remove unnecessary elements.
rules: Each screen should focus on a single primary task.
Avoid more than 5 primary navigation items.
Avoid unnecessary decorative elements.
Reduce text to the minimum required to understand the interface.
Prefer simple layouts over complex multi-column designs.
Use recognizable icons like search, cart, menu, and user profile.
Follow common navigation conventions.
Do not reinvent standard interaction patterns.
Use common UI metaphors when possible.
Prefer established design systems when applicable.

feedback.description: The system must always communicate the result of user actions.
rules: Buttons must visually respond to hover, press, or tap.
Loading indicators must appear during delays.
Successful actions must show confirmation messages.
Errors must clearly explain what happened and how to fix it.
System state changes must be visible.

error_prevention.description: Design should prevent mistakes before they happen.
rules: Validate user input before submission when possible.
Use constrained inputs like dropdowns when appropriate.
Show examples or placeholders in form fields.
Confirm destructive actions such as deletion.
Prevent invalid states through interface constraints.

accessibility.description: Interfaces must be usable by people with different abilities.
rules: Text must meet WCAG contrast requirements.
Font sizes must be readable on small screens.
Interactive elements must be large enough to tap.
Interfaces must support keyboard navigation.
Images must include descriptive alt text.

responsiveness.description: Interfaces must adapt to different screen sizes and devices.
rules: Design mobile-first layouts.
Ensure layouts scale smoothly across breakpoints.
Touch targets must be at least 44px high on mobile.
Avoid horizontal scrolling.
Content must remain readable on all screen sizes.

whitespace.description: Use spacing to improve readability and focus.
rules: Use consistent spacing systems such as 8px grids.
Group related elements together using spacing.
Separate unrelated elements clearly.
Avoid crowded layouts.
Whitespace should guide visual structure.

affordance.description: UI elements must indicate how they can be used.
rules: Clickable elements must visually appear interactive.
Links should be distinguishable from text.
Buttons should visually appear pressable.
Interactive states should provide cues for interaction.
Avoid hidden actions that require guesswork.

recognition_over_recall.description: Users should recognize options rather than remember them.
rules: Provide suggestions or autocomplete when possible.
Display recently used items or history.
Use icons and labels together when helpful.
Expose common actions visibly.
Avoid requiring users to remember information across screens.

progressive_disclosure.description: Reveal complexity gradually to avoid overwhelming users.
rules: Show essential information first.
Hide advanced settings behind expandable sections.
Avoid presenting too many options at once.
Provide contextual help when needed.
Keep advanced functionality accessible but unobtrusive.

primary_action_focus.description: Every screen should guide the user toward a main action.
rules: Each screen must have one dominant call-to-action.
Primary actions must be visually prominent.
Secondary actions must be visually subdued.
Avoid multiple competing primary actions.
Guide user flow toward the intended outcome.

performance_perception.description: Interfaces should feel fast and responsive.
rules: Show loading indicators for operations longer than 300ms.
Use skeleton screens for content loading.
Avoid blocking the interface unnecessarily.
Provide immediate visual response to user input.
Optimize interactions to feel smooth.
Each screen must have one primary action.
Primary buttons must visually stand out.
Secondary actions must be visually less dominant.
Buttons must provide visual feedback on hover and click.
Avoid placing multiple primary buttons in the same section.
